method: Each cohort member is loaded into the event store as a real entity with real events, exactly like any other founder. The backtest then calls the live scorer at successive as_of dates and reads the trajectory off the log. Nothing in this file is a score, a hit rate, or a detection date: those are OUTPUTS and are computed by backtest/runner.py at replay time. If a number the system is supposed to earn appeared here, this would be a retelling, not a backtest.
collection_rule: No source observed after a member's `collection_cutoff` was collected. The cutoff is that member's breakout date, so every event in this file predates the moment the founder became widely known. The replay applies a second, independent cut at read time via as_of.

provenance.reconstructed-from-public-record: Winners are real companies. Their events are reconstructed from public GitHub history, release tags, Hacker News archives and arXiv listings from the stated dates. Payload counts (commit volumes, star counts, contributor counts) are approximations of the public record, not exact API captures — we did not have archival API access to those years. The SHAPE of the history is faithful; individual integers are estimates.
provenance.synthetic: Controls and the deprioritized failure are SYNTHETIC composites, not real companies. They are constructed to be comparably visible to their matched winner while lacking sustained build evidence — which is precisely the confound the H12 fame check exists to detect. A synthetic control is a weaker test than a real one, and this file says so rather than implying otherwise.

threshold.policy: a company clears when the founder axis exceeds 0.62
threshold.calibrated_on: the archetype corpus in data/seed/archetype_*.json, which shares no members with the cohort below

gate_h12.name: fame-vs-trajectory
gate_h12.assertion: no control founder clears the threshold at any replayed as_of
gate_h12.interpretation: Controls are matched contemporaries with comparable visibility. If a control clears, the score is measuring fame rather than trajectory and feature work stops until that is fixed. The PASS/FAIL is computed at replay time from replayed control scores; it is deliberately not recorded here.

winners.name: Docker (dotCloud era)
winners.breakout_description: Docker 1.0 and the funding round that made the name generally known, June 2014.
winners.truncation_note: GitHub history, release tags and Hacker News archives collected only up to 2014-06-01. The first collected signal is the open-sourcing of the container runtime at PyCon, March 2013.

payload.topic: initial open-source drop of the container runtime, lxc driver and README

events.evidence_span: commit "docker: initial public commit — a runtime for building and running containers. See README for docs."
v0.1.0 — first tagged release with a changelog and install docs

payload.title: Show HN: Docker — the Linux container runtime we built inside dotCloud
payload.text: We were running a PaaS and kept rewriting the same containerization layer. We scoped it down to one thing: package a filesystem and a process so it runs the same on any host. Explicitly not a PaaS, not an orchestrator. Trade-off: we chose lxc because writing our own namespace layer would have cost six months, at the cost of some portability.

payload.topic: aufs storage driver, image layer cache, expanded documentation

events.evidence_span: commit "aufs: layered filesystem driver; adds docs/ and a test harness for image builds"
v0.4.0 — "builder: Dockerfile support"; changelog and README rewritten for users

payload.topic: registry client rewrite after the first push/pull design did not work at scale

events.evidence_span: commit "registry: rewrote the push/pull client from scratch; the chunked design did not work above ~200MB images"

payload.text: Author here. Yes, the assumption we made early — that layers would stay small — was wrong, and we reverted the chunked upload path entirely. Pull latency on a 300MB image went from 90s to 22s after the rewrite.

events.evidence_span: founder reply in the registry thread
v0.6.0 — "first release we consider production-shaped"; full changelog

payload.topic: CI pipeline on every pull request, integration test suite, contributor guide

events.evidence_span: commit "ci: run the integration pipeline on every PR; document the release workflow in docs/"

payload.title: Docker 0.7: storage drivers, links, and why we rewrote the runtime layer
payload.text: We benchmarked container start latency at 340ms on the aufs driver and 780ms on devicemapper. The trade-off is that devicemapper works on RHEL where aufs does not, at the cost of roughly 2x start time. Non-goal for 0.7: orchestration across hosts.
Author. The lessons learned from the 0.5 storage rewrite: we should have made the driver interface pluggable before writing the second driver, not after. The postmortem is in the docs.
On the kernel question: we depend on cgroups and namespaces, so anything below 3.8 is out of scope. We state that assumption in the install docs rather than failing at runtime.

payload.topic: libcontainer: replacing the lxc dependency with a native execution driver

events.evidence_span: commit "libcontainer: native namespace/cgroup driver, migrated off lxc; benchmark shows 210ms start"
v0.9.0 — libcontainer becomes the default execution driver; changelog documents the migration

payload.topic: 1.0 hardening: API stability guarantees, docs overhaul, CI pipeline expansion

events.evidence_span: commit "docs: rewrite the API reference ahead of the 1.0 stability guarantee"

winners.name: Hugging Face
winners.breakout_description: The Series A and the point at which the library became the default in the field, spring 2020.
winners.truncation_note: Repository history, release tags, arXiv listings and Hacker News archives collected only up to 2020-05-01. The first collected signal is the pytorch-pretrained-bert release, November 2018.

payload.topic: port of the BERT checkpoints to PyTorch, with a conversion script and README

events.evidence_span: commit "convert_tf_checkpoint_to_pytorch: load the released TF weights; docs in README"

payload.title: Show HN: PyTorch implementations of BERT with the original weights
payload.text: We scoped this narrowly: load the released checkpoints and reproduce the paper numbers, nothing else. Non-goal is a training framework. We match the reported SQuAD F1 to within 0.3%, and the conversion runs in about 40s on CPU.

payload.topic: fine-tuning examples, gradient accumulation, distributed training support

events.evidence_span: commit "examples: run_squad and run_classifier; distributed data parallel path documented"
v0.6.0 — GPT-2 and Transformer-XL added; changelog lists every new architecture

payload.topic: refactored the model API into a common interface before adding more architectures

events.evidence_span: commit "refactor: one PreTrainedModel base class; migrated all six architectures onto it"

payload.topic: renamed the library, added TensorFlow 2 parity, CI workflow across both frameworks

events.evidence_span: commit "ci: github actions matrix over torch and tf2; docs/ restructured for the rename"
v2.0.0 — TensorFlow 2 and PyTorch in one library; changelog documents the migration path

payload.title: HuggingFace's Transformers: State-of-the-art Natural Language Processing
payload.abstract: We present a library of pretrained transformer architectures with a unified API. Code available and open-source at github.com/huggingface/transformers. We report inference throughput of 340 tokens/s on a single V100 for the base model and discuss the trade-off between a shared model interface and architecture-specific optimizations.

payload.text: Author here. We assumed a shared base class would be too restrictive and were wrong — it is what let us add nine architectures in a year. The downside is that architecture-specific kernels are harder to land.
On memory: the base model needs about 1.2 GB for inference at batch 8. We benchmark that in CI so a regression is caught rather than discovered.
Non-goal: we do not try to be a training framework. Explicitly out of scope. We narrowed to loading, fine-tuning and serving pretrained models.

events.evidence_span: v2.5.0 — tokenizers rewritten in Rust; changelog reports 8x speedup on batch encoding

payload.topic: model hub integration, pipelines API, expanded documentation site

events.evidence_span: commit "hub: resolve model ids remotely; docs site rebuilt with per-model changelog"

winners.name: Supabase
winners.breakout_description: The Series A that established the category position, September 2021.
winners.truncation_note: Repository history, release tags and Hacker News archives collected only up to 2021-09-01. The first collected signal is the realtime server going public, January 2020.

payload.topic: Elixir server that listens to Postgres logical replication and broadcasts over websockets

events.evidence_span: commit "replication: decode the WAL stream and fan out over phoenix channels; README explains the assumption that wal_level=logical"

payload.title: Show HN: Realtime Postgres — listen to your database over websockets
payload.text: We narrowed the problem: not a database, not an ORM, just the change stream. Explicitly not a replacement for Postgres replication. Throughput is about 6,000 rps on a single node before we start dropping. Trade-off: we chose logical decoding over triggers at the cost of requiring wal_level=logical.

events.evidence_span: v0.4.0 — row-level filtering; changelog and docker install docs

payload.topic: reconnection handling and a benchmark harness for the change stream

events.evidence_span: commit "bench: sustained 6,000 rps over 30 minutes; docs updated with the tuning guide"
v0.7.0 — per-table subscriptions; changelog with upgrade notes

payload.topic: rewrote the connection pooler after the first approach did not work under concurrent tenants

events.evidence_span: commit "pooler: rewrote per-tenant pooling from scratch; the shared-pool approach did not work above 50 projects"
v0.9.0 — first public beta of the hosted platform; full changelog

payload.text: Author. The lessons learned from the pooler rewrite are written up in our docs: we assumed tenants would be bursty and independent, and they are neither. We rolled back the shared pool entirely.
On lock-in: everything is plain Postgres, so pg_dump is the exit. That is a deliberate trade-off — we give up proprietary features at the cost of nothing we would want anyway.
Cold start on a paused project is around 900ms. We measure it in CI on every release rather than quoting a number we hope is still true.

payload.topic: CI pipeline across every service, generated API docs, self-host documentation

events.evidence_span: commit "ci: workflow runs the integration suite per service; docs auto-generated from the OpenAPI spec"
v1.0.0 — general availability; changelog documents the storage and functions additions

payload.topic: multi-region routing, migrated the dashboard, benchmark suite for query latency

events.evidence_span: commit "bench: p99 query latency 38ms cross-region; migrated the dashboard off the legacy client"
v1.6.0 — edge functions preview; changelog with migration notes

winners.name: Vercel (ZEIT era)
winners.breakout_description: The round and the point at which the framework became a default choice, autumn 2018.
winners.truncation_note: Repository history, release tags and Hacker News archives collected only up to 2018-09-01. The first collected signal is the framework's first public release, October 2016. Attribution note: most of this founder's public output sits on a PROJECT repository rather than a personal one, which is exactly the attribution case the cohort is meant to stress.

payload.topic: server-rendered React framework with filesystem routing; README and examples

events.evidence_span: commit "next: filesystem routing and server-side rendering by default; docs in README"

payload.title: Show HN: Next.js — a minimal framework for server-rendered React
payload.text: We scoped this to one thing: make server rendering the default without a build config. Non-goal is a full application framework; routing is the filesystem and nothing else. Time to first byte on the example app is 48ms.

events.evidence_span: v1.0.0 — first stable release; changelog documents the routing contract

payload.topic: dynamic imports, CI workflow, contributor documentation

events.evidence_span: commit "ci: run the integration pipeline against three node versions; CONTRIBUTING docs added"
v2.0.0 — static export and dynamic imports; changelog with upgrade notes

payload.topic: rewrote the webpack integration after the plugin approach did not work for user config

events.evidence_span: commit "build: rewrote the webpack layer from scratch; the plugin-only approach did not work once users needed custom loaders"

payload.text: Author here. We assumed people would not need to touch webpack and that was wrong; we reverted the sealed-config design. Cold build on the example dropped from 6.2s to 2.8s after the rewrite.

Trade-off on the routing model: filesystem routing gives up programmatic route definitions at the cost of one less concept to learn. We think that is right for the majority case and we say so in the docs.
The lessons learned are in the 3.0 changelog. Short version: we should have exposed the escape hatch in 1.0 rather than defending the abstraction for eight months.

events.evidence_span: v4.0.0 — React 16 and improved code splitting; changelog documents the migration

payload.topic: serverless build target, benchmark harness, documentation site

events.evidence_span: commit "bench: cold start 120ms per serverless route; docs site generated from the same examples the tests run"
v6.0.0 — babel 7, static export improvements; full changelog

payload.topic: webpack 4 migration, docs overhaul, CI expanded to cover the serverless target

events.evidence_span: commit "migrated to webpack 4; ci workflow now covers both build targets"

correctly_deprioritized_failure.name: Veridian Stack
correctly_deprioritized_failure.outcome: Raised a large seed on a well-received deck and shut down 26 months later without a released product.
correctly_deprioritized_failure.why_we_deprioritized: Repository activity is concentrated in three bursts around fundraising dates and near zero in between. Public visibility keeps climbing while shipping evidence does not accumulate. The score follows the artifact rather than the attention.
correctly_deprioritized_failure.why_this_slide_matters: A backtest that only shows the winners it caught is a marketing document. This is the case where the score disagrees with an enthusiastic market — and the disagreement is the point. The score is replayed, not asserted.
correctly_deprioritized_failure.note: Synthetic composite. Collected to 2021-04-01, the date of the round.
